secret_patterns.name: AWS Access Key ID
secret_patterns.reason: AWS Access Key ID exposed - provides full AWS API access.

secret_patterns.name: AWS Secret Access Key
secret_patterns.reason: AWS Secret Access Key exposed - complete compromise of AWS account.

secret_patterns.name: Google API Key
secret_patterns.reason: Google API key exposed - can lead to quota theft and service abuse.

secret_patterns.name: Generic API Key
secret_patterns.reason: Generic API key hardcoded - potential service compromise.

secret_patterns.name: Generic Secret/Token
secret_patterns.reason: Hardcoded secret or password - high risk of unauthorized access.
secret_patterns.context: Virtually never legitimate in production code

secret_patterns.name: GitHub Token
secret_patterns.reason: GitHub token exposed - complete repository access and supply chain risk.

secret_patterns.name: Slack Token
secret_patterns.reason: Slack API token exposed - can send messages, access channels, and exfiltrate data.

secret_patterns.name: Private Key Header
secret_patterns.reason: Complete private key exposed - severe security breach.

secret_patterns.name: Basic Auth in URL
secret_patterns.reason: Username and password embedded in URL - credentials exposed.

secret_patterns.name: Database Connection String
secret_patterns.reason: Database credentials in connection string - immediate data breach risk.

secret_patterns.name: JWT Token
secret_patterns.reason: JWT token hardcoded - session hijacking and impersonation risk.

secret_patterns.name: IP Address (Hardcoded)
secret_patterns.reason: Hardcoded IP address - potential C2 server or infrastructure exposure.
secret_patterns.context: Very common in legitimate code for localhost, test environments

secret_patterns.name: Email Address
secret_patterns.reason: Email address in source - potential PII exposure.
secret_patterns.context: Extremely common - email in comments, docstrings, author info

secret_patterns.name: Azure Storage Account Key
secret_patterns.reason: Azure Storage Account Key exposed - complete storage compromise.

secret_patterns.name: GCP Service Account Key
secret_patterns.reason: GCP service account key exposed - complete GCP environment compromise.

secret_patterns.name: Stripe API Key
secret_patterns.reason: Stripe API key exposed - payment processing and financial data at risk.
secret_patterns.context: Live keys never legitimate; test keys sometimes used

secret_patterns.name: Twilio API Key
secret_patterns.reason: Twilio API key exposed - SMS/call services at risk.

secret_patterns.name: SSH Private Key
secret_patterns.reason: SSH private key exposed - complete server/account compromise.

secret_patterns.name: JWT Secret
secret_patterns.reason: JWT secret exposed - token forgery and session hijacking.

secret_patterns.name: Redis Connection String
secret_patterns.reason: Redis connection with password exposed - data cache breach.

secret_patterns.name: NPM Token
secret_patterns.reason: NPM token exposed - supply chain compromise risk.

secret_patterns.name: SQL Injection — f-string query
secret_patterns.reason: SQL query built with an f-string — user input can escape the query and inject SQL.
secret_patterns.context: SQL queries should use parameterized statements

secret_patterns.name: SQL Injection — % format query
secret_patterns.reason: SQL query built with % formatting — classic injection vulnerability.

secret_patterns.name: SQL Injection — .format() query
secret_patterns.reason: SQL query built with .format() — user-controlled values can inject SQL.

secret_patterns.name: SQL Injection — concatenated query
secret_patterns.reason: SQL query built by string concatenation — injection risk if any part is user-controlled.

secret_patterns.name: Command Injection — shell=True
secret_patterns.reason: subprocess called with shell=True — combined with user input this allows arbitrary command execution.
secret_patterns.context: Avoid shell=True; use list arguments instead

secret_patterns.name: Command Injection — os.system with variable
secret_patterns.reason: os.system() called with a variable — if user-controlled, enables command injection.
secret_patterns.context: Use subprocess with list arguments instead

secret_patterns.name: Weak Crypto — MD5
secret_patterns.reason: MD5 is cryptographically broken — do not use for passwords, signatures, or integrity checks.

secret_patterns.name: Weak Crypto — SHA1
secret_patterns.reason: SHA1 is deprecated for security use — collision attacks are practical.

secret_patterns.name: Weak Crypto — DES
secret_patterns.reason: DES uses a 56-bit key — broken by brute force since the late 1990s.

secret_patterns.name: Weak Crypto — ECB mode
secret_patterns.reason: ECB mode does not hide data patterns — identical plaintext produces identical ciphertext blocks.
secret_patterns.context: Use CBC or GCM mode instead

secret_patterns.name: Weak Crypto — insecure random
secret_patterns.reason: Python random module is not cryptographically secure — use secrets or os.urandom for security purposes.
secret_patterns.context: Use secrets module for security-sensitive randomness

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Deserialization — pickle
secret_patterns.reason: Deserializing untrusted pickle data can execute arbitrary Python code.
secret_patterns.context: Avoid pickle with untrusted data; use JSON instead

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Deserialization — marshal
secret_patterns.reason: marshal.loads on untrusted data can execute arbitrary bytecode.

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Deserialization — yaml.load
secret_patterns.reason: yaml.load() without Loader=yaml.SafeLoader can instantiate arbitrary Python objects.

secret_patterns.name: Path Traversal — open() with variable
secret_patterns.reason: File opened with a variable path — if user-controlled and unsanitized, enables directory traversal.

secret_patterns.name: Path Traversal — join with user input
secret_patterns.reason: os.path.join with apparent user input — attacker can traverse outside intended directory.

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Config — SSL verify=False
secret_patterns.reason: SSL certificate verification disabled — enables man-in-the-middle attacks.
secret_patterns.context: Never disable SSL verification in production

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Config — ssl.CERT_NONE
secret_patterns.reason: ssl.CERT_NONE disables certificate validation — all traffic can be intercepted.

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Config — debug=True
secret_patterns.reason: Debug mode exposes stack traces and internal state to end users.

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Config — HTTP (non-localhost)
secret_patterns.reason: Plaintext HTTP URL — data transmitted without encryption.

secret_patterns.name: Insecure Config — 0.0.0.0 binding
secret_patterns.reason: Server bound to 0.0.0.0 — listens on all interfaces, exposed to external networks.

secret_patterns.name: Anti-Forensics — log/history file reference
secret_patterns.reason: Reference to system log or shell history file — may indicate evidence destruction.
secret_patterns.context: Legitimate logging may reference these files

secret_patterns.name: Anti-Forensics — recursive delete
secret_patterns.reason: Recursive directory deletion — can destroy forensic evidence or wipe payload after execution.
secret_patterns.context: May be legitimate in cleanup operations

secret_patterns.name: Persistence — crontab modification
secret_patterns.reason: Crontab reference — can establish time-based persistence across reboots.

secret_patterns.name: Persistence — startup/init script
secret_patterns.reason: Startup or shell init script reference — can achieve persistence on login/boot.
secret_patterns.context: Legitimate configuration may use these files

secret_patterns.name: Persistence — Windows registry run key
secret_patterns.reason: Windows Registry run key — classic persistence mechanism on Windows.
secret_patterns.context: Legitimate Windows applications may use registry

secret_patterns.name: Persistence — systemd service
secret_patterns.reason: Systemd service installation — can register malicious service to start on boot.

secret_patterns.name: Cryptomining — mining library import
secret_patterns.reason: Known cryptomining library or stratum mining protocol detected.

secret_patterns.name: Cryptomining — mining pool domain
secret_patterns.reason: Known mining pool domain — indicates unauthorized cryptomining activity.
